metadata.from: Bob (Robert) Hanifen — signed 'Your Loving Son, Bob'; return address on envelope reads 'Cpl. Bob Hanifen 17170600, Sqd. T B85-249, Chanute Fld. Ill.'
metadata.to: Hanifen Body & Paint Co., 984 Keo Way, Des Moines, Iowa — addressed to the family business, effectively to his father James Edward Hanifen Sr.
metadata.location_sent_to: Hanifen Body & Paint Co., 984 Keo Way, Des Moines, Iowa
metadata.envelope_notes: Marked 'Free' in upper right corner (military free mail). Return address: Cpl. Bob Hanifen 17170600, Sqd. T B85-249, Chanute Fld. Ill. Addressed to Hanifen Body & Paint Co., 984 Keo Way, Des Moines, Iowa.
people_mentioned: Jimmy (James Edward Hanifen Jr. — Bob's brother, asking if Dad has heard from him)
metadata.mood_tone: Casual, upbeat, and practical — Bob reports on a good weekend, deals with car trouble matter-of-factly, asks for advice, and closes warmly.
metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is generally readable but somewhat hurried. A few words required interpretation: 'Sqd.' on envelope, 'gals' for gallons of gas, 'mirage' likely intended as 'mileage'. Some words on page 2 top lines slightly difficult due to ink flow.
metadata.historical_context: August 1945 — just after the end of WWII (Japan surrendered August 15, 1945). Bob is still stationed at Chanute Field, Illinois. The mention of the ration board reflects wartime rationing still in effect or winding down. Bob inquires about 'Jimmy' — context suggests this is his brother Jim (Jr.), possibly still in service or recently discharged.

pages.transcription:
[Return address, upper left]
Cpl. Bob Hanifen 17170600
Sqd. T  B85-249
Chanute Fld. Ill.

[Postmark]
RANTOUL, ILL.
AUG 21
4-PM
1945

[Upper right]
Free

[Recipient address, center]
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
984 Keo Way

Des Moines, Iowa

U. S. ARMY AIR FORCES

Dear Dad

  I am back at Camp again had a swell weekin. Hitch Hiked up to Chicago. The right rear tire on the Chev. is junk and the spare isn't a lot better. All it does is hold up rear end. I am going down to the ration board now to see about a new ones. they are pretty good about it on the field, so that the only I can figure out how to get

the car home unless you send a tire by mail. Write and tell me what to do. only took 16 gal. of gas and two gts [qts?] of oil. Very good mirage [mileage?]. Have you heard from Jimmy yet, sure hope so. Well Dad goodby for now. Everything is going well
          Your Loving Son
               Bob

full_transcription:
Dear Dad

  I am back at Camp again had a swell weekin. Hitch Hiked up to Chicago. The right rear tire on the Chev. is junk and the spare isn't a lot better. All it does is hold up rear end. I am going down to the ration board now to see about a new ones. they are pretty good about it on the field, so that the only I can figure out how to get the car home unless you send a tire by mail. Write and tell me what to do. only took 16 gal. of gas and two gts [qts?] of oil. Very good mirage [mileage?]. Have you heard from Jimmy yet, sure hope so. Well Dad goodby for now. Everything is going well
          Your Loving Son
               Bob
summary: Bob writes to his father from Chanute Field, Illinois in August 1945, reporting that he had a good weekend and hitchhiked up to Chicago. He explains that the rear tire on the family Chevrolet is worn out and the spare is barely usable, and that he is going to the ration board to try to get new tires; he asks his father to write and advise whether he should send a tire by mail. Bob also notes the car got good gas mileage (16 gallons and 2 quarts of oil for the trip) and inquires whether his father has heard from his brother Jimmy.
